paragraphs.text: Perhaps the most beautiful article called into requisition to adorn the human form divine and especially the female form divine is the warm and downy fur of the sea otter. Few of the aristocratic belles of
on a visit attired in a thousand dollar cloak realize as they pass down
that they are passing over the haunts of a beautiful animal who may have sported the same coat in which they are so snugly ensconced. But such is the fact for the
is the only point of any importance in this industry south of
. Regularly equipped schooners are at all times in the waters of
engaged in hunting these valuable animals, but of all the places along the coast from
claims three quarters of the men and capital engaged in the prosecution of this business. There is but
who have parties out hunting the otter in

The usual and most successful method pursued is to hunt with three boats to a party, each boat containing three men, one or more of whom is a sharp shooter. Rifles of the very finest make are required and some of the weapons brought into requisition are beautiful specimens of the gun maker's art. The firm of
have now three parties upon the Islands of
and others. They are taken over and left upon

, who keep a vessel employed attending to their abalone, seal and otter operations. The skin of an otter is worth all the way from $45 to $500, a wide margin. The points of excellence are size of skin, heaviness and length of hair, and in the best ones called "silver tipped" skins a beautiful color is added to the ordinary shade. The fact that but about 150 skins were obtained in these waters last year and that their value is roughly estimated at in the neighborhood of $10,000, will give an idea of the value and scarcity of the animals. It requires long practice to enable a man to become a successful otter hunter.
About the most expert of the sharp shooters in this business, is
, now dead, was also an expert hunter, and
the first man to shoot otters on this coast
It was on one of these expeditions he discovered the
, whose heroism and exile are celebrated the world over, but whose grave remains unmarked and unhonored in the Nidever lot in

Of the furry prize are among the kelp, where they lie upon their backs, it requiring an experienced eye to distinguish them from sea gulls. They feed upon a shell-fish which grows among the kelp, and of which they are very fond. The work of "bagging" one of these shy, active members of the amphibious order, requires the utmost vigilance, the most practised eye and unlimited patience. When one is seen and fired at, it dives, and the boats endeavor to place themselves in such positions that one of them will be near the game when it comes to the surface to breathe. This may be any time within an hour, as a gentleman experienced in the business assures the representative of

that they can remain as long a time as sixty minutes under the water without requiring air. This seems almost incredible. A mortal shot ends the chase, but as a wounded animal will sometimes occasion a long pursuit, the business has its excitements. The fact that the value of the prize is unknown until it is secured gives the occupation a

As of course a $500 capture, is a lucky hit, and pays for many a day of weary futile watching which is frequently passed. The pursuit of this trade is confined of course to calm weather, as the small and easily handled boats necessary to be used in its prosecution could not live upon a rough sea, and the animals could be neither seen not hit among the white caps. All seasons of the year however, are suitable times, the only distinction being those which are the stormiest, are for that reason and that only the most unsuitable. The fur in its natural state does not resemble the beautiful, soft seal brown color and appearance, which it is generally made to assume after having been in the hands of the furrier. When first taken from the animal the skin has long, somewhat coarse, but still very beautiful hair above the short soft undergrowth which generally alone remains visible in cloaks and hats. , is headquarters for all the peculiar out-of-the-way industries in
. That three-fourths of this one odd but important feature in the word's [world's] commerce that is produced on the

is obtained through the enterprise of Santa Barbara men, speaks well for her business activity and the wonderful variety and value of her resources. It only remains to call attention to the number of people who indirectly profit from the number of men of
who gain a livelihood by this original feature.
